claims.evidenceExcerpt: Why wouldn't we just push that into the graph? Well, I think in a lot of cases that's easier said than done, right?
claims.text: He says moving all warehouse data into graph is often the wrong default because continuous sync, security posture, and custom ETL overhead can make that impractical.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: And so if you notice the thing that we're doing here really is we're not using the graph to copy the data over.
claims.text: He reports that the connection shape uses graph as a semantic layer for metadata, not as a data copy, so the agent can infer joins from structure instead of ingesting the full warehouse.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: Um, and the benefits of having a deterministic load like this is number one, it's going to be item potent.
claims.text: He says the document graph load is deterministic and idempotent, which makes it faster and more repeatable when the source documents already have strong structure.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: I think it would be naive to call it a replacement because most of the people that I see using this will eventually incorporate some sort of hybrid vector retrieval or full text search work I do.
claims.text: He argues that semantic search is not a replacement for the other shapes and that hybrid retrieval, especially with full text search, still matters.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: If the data changes, it will change to reflect the data. So, it's very stable. The disadvantage to it would be if your links in your documents um, and the titles and things that are being scooped up, because this is really only looking basically at document metadata and link metadata.
claims.text: He says the theme view is stable and data-derived, but its usefulness depends on the quality of document titles, links, and metadata.

methods.description: Use a graph semantic layer over warehouse metadata to let an agent reason about joins without copying the underlying tables into graph.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And so we're focused a lot right now on these concepts of ontologies and semantic layers and what we're calling virtual graph where the focus isn't only just etling data in but about okay maybe you want to keep your data where it is and you still want to use SQL and and access it in in the ways you have been but you need some way to sort of guide the agent to be able to do that correctly.
methods.name: Graph semantic layer

methods.description: Load documents deterministically into a containment tree with ordered links so the agent can traverse a table-of-contents view.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So, it's all deterministic with the containment tree for one and then the ordering of the sections as well as the links.
methods.name: Deterministic document ingestion

methods.description: Run full-text search on document and section nodes, then scope results by hierarchical URI so search stays inside the right subtree.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Uh so if I was to go back into this uh file um and I looked at the way that this query run I have this wear statement.
methods.name: URI-scoped search

methods.description: Project the graph into memory and run Leiden community detection to surface clusters and themes efficiently.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So basically what we have to do or what what we do to make it very performant is in addition to just having the database we have this other um projection where we'll take a part of the graph into memory um and then we'll run these high concurrency algorithms on top of that so
methods.name: Projected community detection

methods.description: Write a spec first and have the agent generate Cypher against that spec, rather than hand-authoring complex queries directly.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And I've instructed it inside of the language here to use the Neo Forj cippher skill and reference a spec in doc outline format.m MD.
methods.name: Spec-driven query generation

payload.summary: Zach Blumenfeld argues that lakehouse copilots usually fail because they get the wrong context, not because the model cannot answer questions. His fix is to encode context as reusable graph shapes rather than as a single query style: a semantic-layer graph for warehouse metadata, a deterministic tree-plus-links graph for documents, and community-detection themes for corpus-wide patterns. The practical payoff is that an agent can navigate to the right slice of data, chain the shapes together, and answer both point questions and estate-level coverage questions with less hallucination and less dependence on moving all data into a graph. The main tradeoff is that this lighter-weight approach depends on good source structure and careful naming, so it is faster and more model-agnostic than LLM extraction, but sometimes less expressive and still best combined with search.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: And so there's going to be three concrete shapes that we'll introduce you to today. Uh, so the first one um, we're going to call table of contents and it's somewhat like a tree structure uh, but with also different types of links between them.
takeaways.text: The workshop centers on three reusable shapes: connections, table of contents, and themes.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Um so that's the basics of this first shape. And you can imagine that as your data starts to grow and you start to get more and more tables, it's very useful to have this semantic layer um to then help essentially guide how everything joins together.
takeaways.text: The connection shape gives the agent a semantic layer that helps it join warehouse tables correctly as the schema grows.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Um, that's the general idea with this. And so what this gives the agent to do is not just search like vector search or or lexical search but actually kind of traverse through the documents in a sense.
takeaways.text: The table-of-contents shape lets the agent traverse document hierarchy and links instead of relying only on similarity search.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: And so this is very useful from sort of a whole estate wide question because you can start to understand in your data kind of how everything kind of groups and clusters together.
takeaways.text: Theme detection is useful for estate-level questions because it reveals how documents cluster across the corpus.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: And you'll see what it will do here. Um it will look for the document code. It'll do a full text search.
takeaways.text: The final workflow combines full-text grounding, tree traversal, and warehouse joins so the agent can answer a repair question end to end.
